title: Public Opinion Dynamics regarding Amazon's HQ2 Proposal in Long Island City, New York

polling_trajectory.source: Estimated: Interpolated from initial announcement sentiment shift
Estimated: Gradual erosion due to local activist organizing
Estimated: Holiday lull with persistent negative local press
Estimated: Increased visibility of opposition in City Council hearings
Estimated: Intensified public debate following AOC's vocal opposition
Estimated: Final trend leading to February cancellation

events.description: Amazon officially announced Long Island City, Queens, as one of the two locations for its new headquarters, sparking immediate local debate.
Public reports revealed that New York City and State officials offered Amazon approximately $3 billion in tax incentives to secure the deal.
Local residents and activists held a town hall meeting in Queens to protest the lack of transparency and the potential impact on housing affordability.
Amazon executives testified before the New York City Council, facing intense questioning regarding labor practices and the use of public subsidies.

New York State Senate Majority Leader Andrea Stewart-Cousins appointed vocal Amazon critic Senator Michael Gianaris to the Public Authorities Control Board, giving him potential veto power over the deal.
Amazon officially canceled its plans to build the Long Island City headquarters, citing opposition from local politicians and community members.

agents.name: Holly Sullivan
agents._role: Head of Worldwide Economic Development at Amazon

agents.name: Jeff Wilke
agents._role: CEO of Worldwide Consumer at Amazon

agents.name: Amazon Corporate
agents.description: The company seeking to establish the new headquarters.

agents.name: Local Small Business Owners
agents.description: Concerned about gentrification and rising rents.

agents.name: Tech Industry Workers
agents.description: Generally supportive of job growth and industry expansion.

notes: Pro-side is defined as support for Amazon's HQ2 project in NYC. Against-side is defined as opposition to the project, primarily due to tax incentives and gentrification concerns. Data quality caveat: Only the initial round is based on a high-quality public poll; subsequent rounds are estimates based on the observed trajectory of public discourse leading to the project's cancellation.
